label: ai
topic: Men who confide their trauma to you — loneliness and male vulnerability
text:
The first one was my neighbour, Carl. He caught me taking out the trash on a Tuesday evening and said, without preamble, "My wife left in March and I haven't told anyone." It was October.

Carl was sixty-three. He had a leaf blower he used with religious devotion and a truck he washed every Sunday. He had never said anything more personal than a comment about the weather. Now he was telling me he had been eating dinner alone for seven months, that sometimes he set two places out of habit.

I think I know why he told me. I am a woman in her thirties who makes eye contact and does not interrupt, and this combination is apparently so rare in some men's lives that it functions as a confessional booth.

One in five men reports having no close social connections outside a romantic partner. When the partner leaves, the entire infrastructure of emotional support goes with her.

Carl told me about the leaf blower, which he bought because the sound filled the yard and made the silence afterward feel intentional rather than imposed. He told me he tried a men's group at the community centre but left because a man had cried and Carl had not known where to look.

The second one was my friend Patrick. We were at a bar, the kind where the lighting is forgiving and the music provides cover. Three beers in, he unlocked a memory from college — a night involving an older student, a locked room, and the word no, which he had used and which had not been effective.

"It's not a big deal," he said, which is the sentence men use to introduce the biggest deals of their lives.

He told the story quickly, with the detached efficiency of someone reading directions. He peeled the label off his bottle in thin strips. When he finished, he said, "Anyway," and changed the subject to football, and the conversation closed over the wound like water over a dropped stone.

I said, "I'm sorry that happened to you." He nodded once.

The third one was my father. We were driving home from my aunt's funeral, three hours of highway ahead. He drives with both hands on the wheel, eyes fixed forward — which I now suspect was a way of not having to look at anyone while he spoke.

Somewhere around mile eighty, he told me he had been depressed for most of his forties. Not sad. The clinical kind, which he described as grey. He used to sit in his car in the driveway after work for twenty minutes, because the transition from silence to the noise of the house required energy he did not have.

"I never told your mother."

"Why not?"

"I didn't want to be a burden."

A man sits alone in a car, twenty feet from people who love him, because he has decided his pain is an imposition. He has been taught that needing help is weakness, and weakness in a man is failure.

I think about Carl setting an empty place. Patrick peeling a label. My father in the driveway, gathering strength to walk thirty steps.

They did not want advice or solutions. They wanted a witness.

I hear you.
